title: Revert "Support Windows11 As A sshuttle Server"
body:
Reverts sshuttle/sshuttle#993

This change was made in error. See discussion in #993.

step_plan.summary: Revert the previously-added capability to run sshuttle’s remote server component on Windows 11 by removing Windows-specific server entrypoint behavior, route discovery, and SSH remote-launch support. Keep Windows supported as a client while restoring the pre-change expectation that the sshuttle server runs on a POSIX-like remote host.

steps.operation: Remove/disable Windows-remote-server stdio shimming and deferred-exit behavior in the remote entrypoint, restoring the pre-#993 POSIX-only server startup expectations (or explicitly erroring on win32 when running as a server).
steps.reason: #996 is a revert of Windows 11 *server* support; the main remote entrypoint is where Windows-specific mux/stdin-stdout adaptations and termination hooks were added to make the server run on Windows.
steps.side_effects: Windows will no longer be a supported *remote server* target; ensure Windows client mode remains unaffected. May change failure mode when attempting to run server on win32 (e.g., earlier explicit error). Verify POSIX remote server operation over SSH still works.

steps.operation: Remove/guard the Windows route-table discovery path (e.g., `route PRINT -4`) so server-side `--auto-nets` no longer attempts Windows route parsing when running on win32; restore prior behavior (likely non-Windows parsing only, or an unsupported-platform error).
steps.reason: Windows route parsing is part of enabling Windows as a remote server with `--auto-nets`. Reverting Windows server support should remove or disable Windows-only route discovery on the server side.
steps.side_effects: `--auto-nets` will not work on a Windows remote server (by design after revert). Ensure non-Windows route discovery continues to function and that callers handle unsupported platform behavior cleanly.

steps.operation: Remove the Windows-specific route parsing helper or make it unreachable behind an explicit unsupported-platform guard (consistent with the revert), and clean up any now-dead references.
steps.reason: If Windows server mode is no longer supported, `_route_windows` becomes dead code or a maintenance burden; reverting should either delete it or prevent it from being used.
steps.side_effects: Any internal references from `list_routes` must be updated accordingly. If left in place but unreachable, confirm it does not trigger lint/test failures for unused code in this repo’s tooling.

steps.operation: Revert remote-launch behavior that enables Windows as the *remote server* (e.g., cmd/powershell remote shell handling and/or Windows-specific stdio plumbing), restoring prior restriction that remote servers are POSIX-like, or explicitly erroring when a Windows remote-shell/server is requested.
steps.reason: `connect()` constructs the remote command line and transport for running `sshuttle.server`. Windows-server enablement commonly requires cmd/powershell quoting and pipe semantics; reverting should remove that support path and prevent Windows remote server execution.
steps.side_effects: Users relying on `--remote-shell cmd|powershell` for Windows remote targets will lose that functionality (intended by revert). Ensure this does not break normal OpenSSH usage on Windows as a *client* connecting to a POSIX server.

steps.operation: Adjust the shim initialization contract so it no longer contains Windows-server-specific behavior (e.g., special `on_end`/deferred-exit expectations), and ensure it is only used where still needed (likely Windows client-side pipe selectability, not remote server).
steps.reason: `SocketRWShim` is a key enabling utility for Windows pipe/stdin-stdout bridging. Reverting Windows server support may require removing server-specific shutdown hacks or narrowing the shim’s intended use back to client-side needs.
steps.side_effects: If other modules still rely on the shim (especially Windows client code paths), behavior must remain compatible. Threading/cleanup semantics changes may affect process shutdown timing; run existing tests and perform a basic Windows-client smoke check if available.

steps.operation: Update shim file-wrapper behavior if it was modified to satisfy Windows server muxing (e.g., binary/text modes, buffering, close semantics) so it aligns with remaining supported use cases; ensure callers can still pass the returned file objects into mux code reliably.
steps.reason: `makefiles()` is the handoff point to the mux layer; Windows-server support may have required specific file-like behavior. After reverting, keep only what’s necessary for supported platforms/modes and avoid server-only hacks.
steps.side_effects: May impact mux I/O behavior on Windows client or other environments using the shim. Validate that mux framing still works and that no regressions occur in normal POSIX remote server operation.
